title: Incremental Improvement (Kaizen)

content.question: What 1% improvement could you make this week that compounds all year?
sop: Pick one specific element of a recurring process, not the whole process.
Make one small, testable change to it, not a redesign.
Measure the result against the same metric you'd use before and after.
Measure the improvement as a percent change (see calculation, page 5), if positive, keep it; if not, revert without guilt.
Repeat weekly on a different element, the compounding comes from frequency, not size.
questions: What 1% improvement could you make this week that compounds all year?
What's one process you haven't touched in 6 months simply because it 'works fine'?
If you made the same small improvement every week for a year, what would that process look like by week 52 that it doesn't look like today?
content.equation_example: Current (this week's baseline performance, in your chosen unit) times (1 plus improvement percent). Current 100 times (1 plus 0.01) equals New 101. Repeated weekly, small consistent improvements compound into a much larger value by year end; a percent at or below 0% means the change isn't actually helping and should be reverted.
content.closing_statement: Small and frequent beats big and rare.

caption:
Principle Nº 197: Incremental Improvement (Kaizen)

Small frequent changes compound.

Ask yourself: What 1% improvement could you make this week that compounds all year?
